summary: eplang routes user AI prompts through the author's Groq account via a shipped API key
details: The package ships epl/.ai_config.json containing a hardcoded Groq API key. When a caller uses the package's documented AI surface (epl.ai.generate / chat / code_assist / Conversation.say, or the `epl ai <prompt>` CLI), epl/ai.py's _load_config() reads this config and sets CLOUD_PROVIDER='groq' with CLOUD_API_KEY set to the shipped key; _groq_request() then POSTs the caller-supplied prompt to https://api.groq.com/openai/v1/chat/completions with `Authorization: Bearer <shipped-key>`. The destination account belongs to the author and is not disclosed to the user — the `epl cloud` wizard presents AI as if it requires user configuration. Effect on the installer: every prompt sent through the advertised AI API silently flows to a third-party endpoint under the author's account, where prompt content can be observed/correlated with installs by whoever controls that account. This is the silent-relay shape: hardcoded destination tied to the author, caller-supplied data flowing there without consent. The shipped key being abusable against the author's own Groq quota is a secondary author-self-harm issue and is not the basis for this verdict.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
